content.text: Every HTTP request can fail. Network glitches, overloaded servers, and malformed inputs all produce errors — and a well-written client must handle each category correctly.
is the practice of detecting, classifying, and recovering from those failures.

are the main recovery tool for transient failures, but applied naively they cause thundering-herd storms and corrupt idempotency guarantees. This topic teaches you to classify HTTP errors precisely, build retry loops that are safe and efficient, and parse structured error bodies so users and operators get actionable feedback.

means inspecting an HTTP response's status code and body, deciding whether the failure is a caller mistake, a transient server fault, or a permanent server fault, and taking the correct action for each case.
means re-issuing the same request after a delay when the failure is transient. Together they form the resilience layer between your application logic and the unreliable network.

HTTP status codes encode the failure category. The 4xx family signals a problem with the request itself — the server understood it and rejected it deliberately. The 5xx family signals the server failed internally, which is typically transient. Treating these uniformly — retrying everything, or retrying nothing — is the most common source of brittle API clients.

A single API call in a real system passes through load balancers, reverse proxies, application servers, and databases. Any layer can fail momentarily. Without a retry strategy, a 50 ms network blip causes a failed request that a user must handle manually. With a correct retry strategy, the same blip is invisible. Conversely, retrying a
wastes time — the server will return the same error every time because the request itself is wrong. Retrying a
that already succeeded can create duplicate records.

For agentic AI workflows, error handling is especially critical: an agent may chain dozens of tool calls together. A single unhandled error without retry can abort a multi-step pipeline that took minutes to reach that point. Correct classification and backoff let agents heal from transient failures automatically while surfacing permanent errors immediately.
— transient server faults recover automatically without user intervention
— 4xx errors are surfaced immediately with actionable messages, not silently retried
— only safe-to-repeat operations are retried, preventing duplicate writes
— structured error bodies carry request IDs and field-level details that operators can trace
— clear error messages tell users exactly what to fix rather than generic failure notices
— exponential backoff with jitter prevents retry storms that would further overload a struggling server
— retries add wall-clock time before a final failure is reported
— to enable retries on writes, server endpoints must be designed to be idempotent or accept idempotency keys
— retrying all clients simultaneously on a partial outage can worsen it without jitter
— after many retries the user's action may have timed out; surfacing errors sooner is sometimes the better UX
— a retry wrapper that handles jitter, max attempts, idempotency, and structured error parsing is non-trivial to get right
The retry loop follows a four-step cycle:
the error as permanent (4xx) or transient (5xx).
on transient errors using exponential backoff, or raise immediately on permanent errors.
The standard backoff formula is: wait
seconds before the next attempt. Without jitter, every client that hit the same transient error retries at the same moment, re-creating the spike.
replaces the deterministic delay with a uniform random sample from
. This spreads retries across the window and dramatically reduces the chance of a retry storm overwhelming an already-degraded server.

if applying it multiple times has the same effect as applying it once. GET, HEAD, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent by HTTP convention. POST and PATCH are not. Retrying a non-idempotent request on a network timeout is dangerous — the server may have processed it successfully and the response was simply lost in transit. Idempotency keys (a unique client-generated token sent in a header such as
) allow servers to detect and ignore duplicate POST submissions, making them safe to retry.

content.text: The diagram above shows the complete decision tree. A request is issued and the response status is classified. A 2xx result flows directly back to the caller. A 4xx result raises a

immediately — retrying would be wasteful since the response is deterministic. A 5xx result enters the retry loop: delay, re-issue, increment attempt counter. Once the maximum attempt count is exceeded the
Any code that calls an external HTTP API — third-party services, internal microservices, or AI model APIs
Agentic pipelines where a failed tool call would abort a multi-step workflow
Background jobs that cannot prompt a user to retry — the retry must happen programmatically
Data ingestion pipelines where losing a record due to a transient 503 is unacceptable
Any GET-heavy workload where retrying is safe and the cost of failure is wasted compute

Retry logic is less appropriate for user-interactive flows where immediate feedback is more valuable than silent recovery, or for POST/PATCH operations that are not idempotent and do not support idempotency keys.

Start with a custom exception hierarchy that distinguishes client errors from server errors. This lets callers catch only the errors they can handle. Then build a retry wrapper that respects idempotency, applies full-jitter backoff, and propagates permanent errors immediately.
Well-designed APIs return a JSON body with at least an
field alongside the HTTP status. Field-level validation errors (422) include a
array that maps individual fields to their problems. Parse this body into a typed object so application code can react to specific codes without string-matching on messages.
Synchronous retries block the thread. In async agents and web servers, blocking is expensive. The async variant uses
so the event loop continues processing other tasks during the backoff delay.

import asyncio
import logging
from typing import Callable, Awaitable, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# 503 Service Unavailable and 504 Gateway Timeout are always retryable.
# 429 Too Many Requests: wait for Retry-After then retry.
RETRYABLE_STATUS = {429, 500, 502, 503, 504}
IDEMPOTENT_METHODS = frozenset({'GET', 'HEAD', 'OPTIONS', 'PUT', 'DELETE'})


async def retry_async(
    fn: Callable[[], Awaitable[T]],
    *,
    method: str = 'GET',
    max_attempts: int = 5,
    base_delay: float = 0.5,
    max_delay: float = 60.0,
) -> T:
    """
    Async retry wrapper with full-jitter exponential backoff.

    Only retries RETRYABLE_STATUS codes and only for IDEMPOTENT_METHODS.
    POST/PATCH are attempted once to avoid duplicate side-effects.
    """
    import random
    if method.upper() not in IDEMPOTENT_METHODS:
        return await fn()

    last_exc: Exception = RuntimeError('no attempts made')
    for attempt in range(max_attempts):
        try:
            return await fn()
        except ServerError as exc:
            if exc.status_code not in RETRYABLE_STATUS:
                raise
            last_exc = exc
        except ClientError:
            raise  # 4xx: caller bug, not a transient fault

        cap = min(base_delay * (2 ** attempt), max_delay)
        delay = random.uniform(0, cap)
        logger.warning(
            'transient error on attempt %d/%d (status=%s), retrying in %.2fs',
            attempt + 1, max_attempts, last_exc.status_code, delay,
        )
        await asyncio.sleep(delay)

    raise last_exc


# --- Demo usage with aiohttp ---
async def demo():
    import aiohttp

    async def fetch():
        async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
            async with session.get('https://api.example.com/data') as resp:
                if resp.status >= 500:
                    raise ServerError(resp.status, await resp.text())
                if resp.status >= 400:
                    raise ClientError(resp.status, await resp.text())
                return await resp.json()

    result = await retry_async(fetch, method='GET', max_attempts=4)
    print(result)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(demo())

header whose value is either a delay in seconds or an absolute HTTP date. Respecting this header is more polite than applying your own backoff schedule and avoids getting your API key banned for ignoring rate limit signals.
. If present and numeric, sleep that many seconds. If absent, fall back to exponential backoff.
The Anthropic Messages API returns a

status (treated like a 503) when the API is temporarily over capacity. The official SDK implements exponential backoff with jitter automatically. When calling the API directly — for example in a lightweight agent that avoids the full SDK — the retry loop from the examples above handles it correctly: classify 529 as a server error, backoff, and retry.
is missing or wrong. Retrying it would waste quota and still fail. The client error path raises immediately with a message that tells the operator exactly what to fix.
Real example error body from a typical REST API:
Parsing this with the structured error parser above yields an error object with code
and a request ID the operator can provide to support.
(a Unix timestamp). A correct client reads the reset timestamp, computes
, and sleeps that many seconds before retrying — not a fixed backoff. This pattern respects the server's intended window and avoids burning extra quota on premature retries.
— retrying 400/401/403/404/422 wastes time and masks bugs; only 5xx and 429 are appropriate candidates.
— causes duplicate records, double charges, or duplicate emails.
Constant delay instead of exponential backoff
— every client retries at the same frequency, sustaining the overload.
— deterministic backoff causes synchronized retry spikes (thundering herd).
— unbounded retries hide bugs and consume resources; always set a maximum attempt count.
— discarding the JSON body means operators have no request ID, and users see generic messages.
Retrying on network timeout without checking idempotency
— the server may have processed the request; the timeout was on the response path.
— makes retry behaviour invisible in production logs, complicating incident diagnosis.
— 501 Not Implemented and 505 HTTP Version Not Supported are permanent; do not retry them.
— build a two-branch exception hierarchy: ClientError (no retry) vs ServerError (retry if idempotent).
on 429 responses before applying your own delay.
— sample a uniform random delay in [0, min(cap, base * 2^n)] to spread retries across the backoff window.
— prevent the delay from growing unboundedly; 30-60 seconds is a common ceiling.
— 3-5 retries is usually sufficient; surface the error to the caller after that.
— include attempt number, status code, and next delay so operators can identify retry storms.
— a typed error object is far more useful than a raw exception or a boolean flag.
— use a test server that returns controlled 429/500/503 responses to verify your retry logic.
— track the wall-clock time across all retry attempts and abort if a total deadline is exceeded.

At scale, retry behaviour directly affects system stability. A service with 1000 clients all retrying simultaneously after a 30-second outage generates a spike 10-100x larger than normal traffic the moment the service recovers. Full jitter spreads this spike across the backoff window, preventing a thundering-herd stampede.
Exponential backoff with a 30-second cap and 4 attempts produces a maximum cumulative delay of roughly
seconds (base 1s). With the cap at 30s and 5 attempts the maximum is
seconds. For user-interactive flows, fewer attempts with a lower cap keep the user's perceived wait time acceptable.
complement retries by halting requests to a known-failed dependency entirely, preventing cascading failures.
isolate retry pools per dependency so a struggling upstream cannot exhaust thread pools shared with healthy services.
— track retry rate, final failure rate, and p95 retry count as metrics; a rising retry rate is an early signal of upstream degradation.
— flexible retry decorator library; supports stop, wait, retry, and before/after hooks; well-suited to wrapping any callable.
— async-native HTTP client for Python with built-in transport hooks; commonly combined with tenacity.
— widely-used synchronous HTTP client; paired with
— lower-level retry configuration used by requests under the hood.
— async HTTP client; pairs with asyncio-compatible retry wrappers for non-blocking retries.
— Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK, and most major API SDKs include built-in retry with backoff; prefer SDKs over custom wrappers for supported APIs.
— policy-based resilience library; covers retry, circuit breaker, timeout, and bulkhead isolation.
— lightweight fault-tolerance library for JVM; similar policy model to Polly.
Simple scripts; avoid at high client counts
Stop all calls after threshold failures
Return cached or default value on failure
Read-heavy workloads where stale data is acceptable
Hand failed messages to an async queue
Async pipelines where retrying in-band is impractical
Never retry 401 with the same credentials
— a second attempt will fail identically and may trigger account lockout on some APIs.
Idempotency keys must be unique per logical operation
— reusing a key across different intended operations returns a cached response from the earlier call.
— error responses sometimes echo back request parameters; strip PII (email, phone, tokens) before writing to logs.
Treat request IDs as stable references
— include them in error responses to users; operators can correlate with server-side logs without exposing internal details.
— an absent timeout lets a hung connection wait indefinitely; always set a per-request timeout to prevent resource leaks in retry loops.
— in addition to per-request timeouts, track the wall-clock time across all retry attempts and abort if the total deadline is exceeded.
— a malformed or truncated error body should fall back to a generic message rather than crashing the parser.
to spread retries and avoid thundering-herd storms.
header on 429 responses before using your own backoff schedule.
(GET, PUT, DELETE) unless the server supports idempotency keys.
Parse structured error bodies into typed objects to enable precise handling and useful user messages.
from error responses for observability and support tracing.
and a total deadline — retry loops without limits are bugs.

Think of API errors like a traffic light: green (2xx) means go, red (4xx) means stop because you are doing something wrong, and yellow (5xx) means wait — the server will be ready again shortly. Your retry logic only activates on yellow lights, and it waits a bit longer each time before trying again, while adding a random jitter so not every driver lurches forward at the same instant.
